title: 8.9 practice
description: Practice what you learned in lesson 8.9!

assessment_items.question_content:
**In Ghana, why was much of the anti-colonial resistance actually directed at chiefs and kings?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Chiefs and kings acted as the main local representatives of British rule.
Chiefs and kings were criticized for not resisting British control more strongly.
Chiefs and kings were blamed for mishandling taxes and land distribution.
Chiefs and kings were thought to have surrendered the Golden Stool to the British.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What kinds of resistance methods are difficult to track in the historical record?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: informal resistance
armed struggle
boycotts
indirect rule

assessment_items.question_content:
**What was Kwame Nkrumah’s strategy of “positive action”?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Positive action relied on nonviolence and noncooperation to challenge colonial rule.
Positive action relied on violent resistance to remove British authority.
Positive action required accommodating British officials to gain gradual independence.
Positive action encouraged Ghanaians to adopt British culture through assimilation.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What impact did World War II have on the rise of independence movements in Ghana?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Ghanaian veterans returned from the war to hardship and demanded political change.
World War II had little effect on Ghanaians because they did not participate in the war.
World War II encouraged Britain to invest more resources in Ghana to improve living conditions.
World War II caused fascism to take hold in Ghana, leading Britain to abandon the colony.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which ideology helped many colonies build a shared cultural identity and overcome ethnic divisions during the independence movements?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Nationalism
Socialism
Communism
Pan-Islamism

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did independence movements in French colonies differ from those in British colonies?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: French colonial independence movements were more likely to turn violent, while British colonies often used nonviolent protests.
France usually negotiated peacefully, while Britain resisted independence with military force.
French colonies experienced primarily nonviolent movements, while British colonies saw widespread violent uprisings.
French colonies gained independence after World War I, while Britain held India until 1990 for economic reasons.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Why was national unity difficult to achieve in many postcolonial countries?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Many groups had been forced together under colonial borders, creating tensions after independence.
Newly independent nations were not recognized by the international community.
Former colonizers continued to provoke conflict among different groups.
Colonial borders kept culturally similar groups together, making unity easy to achieve.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did the partition of British India lead to one of the largest migrations in history?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Partition split India and Pakistan by religion, triggering mass migration between the two new countries.
Millions fled India after independence because the economy collapsed and people sought better opportunities.
Britain forced the Hindu majority to leave India as part of the independence agreement.
Many people preferred Western culture and migrated to the United States after Britain withdrew.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Why did African women rarely appear in the official records kept during colonial rule?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Colonial rule kept most African women out of public roles, so they were not documented.
African women preferred domestic roles over public or political positions.
African women were not interested in having official records of their work.
African women refused to be documented as a form of protest against colonial rule.

assessment_items.question_content:
How did the women of southeastern Nigeria respond to the British system of taxation?

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: They used traditional protest methods as well as violence to attack and destroy colonial buildings.
They largely supported the tax, hoping acceptance would gain them more rights under British rule.
They refused to protest out of fear of British retaliation.
They organized widespread boycotts as a non-violent way to convince the British to remove the tax.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did the roles of African women under European colonial rule differ from their roles in pre-colonial societies?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Before colonialism, many African women had power and trade roles, but Europeans limited them to domestic duties.
Before colonialism, women were limited to domestic roles, but under colonialism they gained political and economic power.
Before colonialism, women were mostly domestic workers, and colonial rule kept their roles the same.
Before colonialism, many women had leadership roles, but these roles stayed the same under colonial rule.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What was one long-term effect of European colonization on African women’s roles and rights?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Colonization reduced women’s traditional authority and strengthened male-dominated structures.
Colonial discriminatory laws disappeared after independence, giving women full legal equality.
Women kept their traditional economic roles, which allowed them to gain financial independence after independence.
Europeans prioritized women’s education, creating long-term gains in literacy and representation.
